---
title: "Divine Policy: The Impact of Religion in Government"
author: "Bentzen, J. S., Pizzigolotto, A., Sperling, L. L."
date: "2025"
date-format: "YYYY"
description: "staggered binary DiD: faith-based-initiative adoption on religious-nonprofit prevalence"
categories:
  - "DID → DID_PLR"
  - "Religion & Policy (DiD)"
  - "2025"
  - "Extension demo"
image: forest_plot.png
paper-journal: "American Economic Journal: Applied Economics"
paper-doi: "10.1257/app.20240018"
recast-path: "DID → DID_PLR"
replication-regime: "stochastic"
---

## Summary

**Citation:** Bentzen, J. S., Pizzigolotto, A., Sperling, L. L. (2025). *Divine Policy: The Impact of Religion in Government*. *American Economic Journal: Applied Economics*. [doi:10.1257/app.20240018](https://doi.org/10.1257/app.20240018)

A staggered BINARY DiD that stress-tested the pipeline. DoubleMLDIDMulti (Callaway–Sant'Anna) is infeasible on a 50-state panel, so the pipeline gracefully fell back to the DML two-way-FE estimator (loudly flagged). The effect on religious-nonprofit share is positive but underpowered: TWFE +0.0016 (t=1.4); DML +0.0018–0.0025, significant for one of three learners.

::: {.glance}
<div class="g"><span class="k">Method path</span><span class="v">DID → DID_PLR</span></div>
<div class="g"><span class="k">Replication</span><span class="v">stochastic</span></div>
<div class="g"><span class="k">Review verdict</span><span class="v">Extension demo</span></div>
<div class="g"><span class="k">Identification</span><span class="v">DID</span></div>
:::

## Estimand & identification

{{< include estimand_statement.md >}}

## Replication

**Regime:** stochastic · **Gate:** PASS · **Overall tier:** PARTIAL

No deterministic published target is available, so replication is a documented *partial*. Our estimate: **0.0016** (SE 0.0011), n = 1050.

[No deterministic published target reproduced (paper paywalled; .do build requires Stata; GSS outcome restricted). Replication is a documented PARTIAL on the sign + significance of the two-way FE staggered DiD on religious-nonprofit share.]{.text-muted-sm}

## The gap table

Original result, our replication/extension, the published benchmark (where one exists), and the verdict. The estimator never saw the benchmark — it is compared only after the results were frozen.


**Staggered DiD (religious-NPO share ~ FBI adoption)**

| Estimator | Original | Ours | Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TWFE DiD (replication) | not extractable (no Stata; GSS outcome restricted) | 0.0016 (se 0.0011, t 1.43) | none (2025 paper; no revisit) | PARTIAL |

**DML extension (DID -> DID_PLR fallback)**

| Estimator | Original | Ours | Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DML - Forest | - | 0.0022 (se 0.0011, p 0.044) | none | consistent |
| DML - Lasso | - | 0.0018 (se 0.0011, p 0.109) | none | tension |
| DML - Boosting | - | 0.0025 (se 0.0013, p 0.056) | none | tension |

*Verdict counts:* PARTIAL 1, consistent 1, tension 2.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Note
Callaway & Sant'Anna (DoubleMLDIDMulti) was infeasible at this sample size (small cross-section / too few never-treated units / empty (g,t) cells); reported estimate is the DML two-way-FE DiD on a treated x post indicator. This is subject to the TWFE-under-staggered-heterogeneity caveat that CS2021 addresses -- interpret as an approximate average ATT, not the CS group-time estimate. GSS attitudes outcome is restricted (not in the package); this recast uses the religious-nonprofit (NCCS) share outcome that IS included. DoubleMLDIDMulti was infeasible at 50 states, so the DML two-way-FE fallback is reported (flagged).
:::


## Causal-ML extension

![Staggered DiD: TWFE replication vs the DML extension (DoubleMLDIDMulti infeasible at 50 states → DID_PLR fallback) across learners (95% CIs).](forest_plot.png)

Per-learner numbers are in the gap table above. 
All learners are the DML difference-in-differences estimator described above.

## What causal ML added

This run is valuable precisely because it found a limit. The design is staggered adoption of state faith-based initiatives, so the router routes it to DoubleMLDIDMulti. But with only 50 states (6 never-treated, several single-state cohorts) the Callaway–Sant'Anna ML estimator hits empty per-(g,t) nuisance cells and cannot be fit. Rather than crash, the pipeline falls back to the DML two-way-FE estimator on a treated × post indicator and labels it loudly — because TWFE under staggered heterogeneity is exactly what Callaway–Sant'Anna exists to correct. The estimate is directionally consistent with the paper (faith-based initiatives → more faith-based nonprofits) but small and significant for only one learner at this sample size. The paper's headline GSS attitudes outcome lives in restricted files not shipped in the package, so this recast targets the religious-nonprofit outcome that is included.

## AI peer review

This run is a **replication + causal-ML extension demonstration** of the difference-in-differences pathway; it has not yet been put through the two-referee AI review (unlike the studies above).

## Downloads & reproducibility

- Data: state×year panel reconstructed in Python from the openICPSR raw files (Sager faith-based-law timing + the 14.4M-row NCCS nonprofit panel) by the project's `build_project.py`; the GSS attitudes outcome is restricted and not included.
- Full result artifacts (gap table, frozen estimates, referee reports) live in the project's `data/results/` and `paper/review_history/`.
